rulings.Case Title: IN RE: JOHN DOE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petitioner John Does Petition To Quash Out-Of-State Administrative Subpoena Request Form To Google Llc Or Alternatively For A Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 1, PETITIONER JOHN DOE'S Petition to Quash Out-Of-State Administrative Subpoena Request Form to Google LLC or Alternatively for a Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge Sigrid Irias, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to Sigrid.irias@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GEOFFREY MURRY VS. JOHN T. HENDRICKS ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp 128.7
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF GEOFFREY MURRY MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp 128.7. Plaintiff Geoffrey Murry's motion for sanctions is granted. Pursuant to Code of Civ. Proc., § 128.7(b)(3), Hendricks' factual contentions must have evidentiary support or are likely to have such support. The record shows that although the parties contemplated that Murry would become a shareholder in the future, going so far as to change the name of the firm and enter into an interim stock purchase agreement, at all times the law firm was a professional corporation and Murry never actually became a de jure shareholder. (Cohorn Decl., Exs. A - E.) The corporate minutes, signed by Hendricks, establish that Hendricks consistently owned 100% of the outstanding shares. (Id. at Ex. E ["John T. Hendricks, the Corporation's sole shareholder".) Hendricks nevertheless alleges in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint that Murry was, in fact, a "partner and shareholder" in the firm. (E.g., TAC 10, 11, 15, 16, 35, 37, 45, 56, 57, 58, 60.) Moreover, other allegations in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint show that Hendricks knew that Murry never became a shareholder. Hendricks admits that Murry never paid the $1,000 to purchase the shares. (TAC, 10, 13["Murry never funded his check for his initial stock purchase of $1,000 and he never made an additional stock purchase."].) A number of claims against Murry in the Third Amended Cross-Complaint are based on that false allegation. The court strikes the Third Amended Cross-Complaint with leave to amend. Hendricks is instructed to remove all allegations that allege that Murry was in fact a "partner or shareholder." Given the court's policy to hear cases on the merits, Hendricks has 20 days leave to allege claims that are consistent with the factual record. Both parties' requests for monetary sanctions are denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILENA LEVITIN VS. PHUKAB MONTAKARN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MONTAKARN PHUKAB, PRASOMSUK LLC, SIAM ORCHID TRADITIONAL THAI MASSAGE DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Montakarn Phukab's demurrer to plaintiff Milena Levitin's second amended complaint (SAC) is overruled. On demurrer, the Court accepts as true properly pled allegations of fact in the complaint as well as facts which may be inferred from those expressly alleged. (Mez Industries, Inc. v. Pacific Nat. Ins. Co. (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 856.) Defendant improperly alleges facts regarding Bhawana Thai Massage, LLC, that are not found in the SAC and as to which defendant has not properly sought judicial notice. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 430.70; Cal. R. Ct. 3.1306(c).) Plaintiff was granted leave to amend in order to allege sufficient facts to support naming defendant Ms. Phukab personally liable for the alleged negligence of DOE 1. The SAC alleges the following: (1) Ms. Phukab "owned and operated Defendant Siam Orchid Traditional Thai Massage as a sole proprietorship or general partnership" (SAC 1); (2) Ms. Phukab owned and operated the business as a sole proprietorship or general partnership and is liable to plaintiff "by virtue of respondeat superior and/or agent-principle [sic] liability pursuant to Cal. Civ. Code § 2338" (SAC 3); (3) Ms. Phukab is directly liable "to plaintiff for negligent hiring, retention, and supervising of Defendant DOE 1" (SAC 4); and (4) Ms. Phukab remains personally liable pursuant to Cal. Corp. Code § 17710.09(c)(1)" (SAC 5-6). Plaintiff is entitled to plead facts or legal theories in the alternative, or even inconsistently. (See Radar Co. v. Stone (1986) 178 Cal.App.3d 10, 29.) The SAC sufficiently pleads allegations that would support holding defendant Ms. Phukab personally liable as a sole proprietor. (See e.g. Twenty-Nine Palms Enterprises Corp. v. Bardos (2012) 210 Cal.App.4th 1435, 1449 "[a] sole owner is a sole proprietorship and a sole proprietorship is not a legal entity separate from its individual owner."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: MILENA LEVITIN VS. PHUKAB MONTAKARN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT MONTAKARN PHUKAB, PRASOMSUK LLC, SIAM ORCHID TRADITIONAL THAI MASSAGE MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Montakarn Phukab, Prasomsuk LLC, and Siam Orchid Traditional Thai Massage's motion to strike plaintiff Milena Levitin's second amended complaint is denied. As explained in the Court's order on the demurrer, plaintiff has sufficiently alleged a cause of action for negligence against Ms. Phukab. As with demurrers, the grounds for a motion to strike must appear on the face of the complaint or from matter which is properly the subject of judicial notice. (Code Civ. Proc., § 437.) rulings.Case Title: FERMIN P MATURIN ET AL VS. GENERAL MOTORS LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses From Defendant, And Request For Sanctions - First Set Of Request For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 7, PLAINTIFFS FERMIN MATURIN AND LETICIA MATURIN'S Motion to Compel Further Discovery Responses from Defendant, and Request for Sanctions - First Set of Request for Production of Documents. Pro Tem Judge Sigrid Irias, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion denied on the grounds that it was not timely served. The motion was brought pursuant to CCP section 2031.310. Subsection (d) of that section provides in part that unless notice of the motion is given within 45 days of the service of the response, or any supplemental response, or on or before any specific later date to which the demanding party and the responding party have agreed in writing, the demanding party waives any right to compel a further response to the inspection demand. The moving papers state that the original responses were served June 6, 2019. The papers filed do not establish that an extension of time in which to file a motion to compel further responses was granted, but state that a request for an extension that was sent after the filing deadline had expired was not responded to. The proof of service for the moving papers states that they were served August 20, 2019, more than 45 days after June 6, 2019. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to Sigrid.irias@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JORDAN ONG VS. BEENEST, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT BEENEST, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION, JONATHAN CHOU MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' motion to strike is denied. Defendants' motion to strike Plaintiff's request for punitive damages is denied. The Court has overruled Defendants' demurrer to the fraud cause of action. Defendants' motion to strike plaintiff's request for attorney's fees is denied. Code of Civil Procedure § 1021.5 authorizes an award of attorney's fees to successful parties in actions that have "resulted in the enforcement of an important right affecting the public interest." It is essential to such an award that "a significant benefit, whether pecuniary or nonpecuniary, has been conferred on the general public or a large class of persons" and that "the necessity and financial burden of private enforcement . . . are such as to make the award appropriate." (Code Civ. Proc. § 1021.5(a),(b).) It is doubtful whether this action for unpaid wages and breach of contract, which is brought on behalf of a single individual plaintiff and seeks to recover substantial monetary damages, will be able to satisfy either element. However, because a prayer for fees under § 1021.5 need not be pled at all, and it is premature to determine whether the bases for an award can be met, the motion to strike is denied. (See Snatchko v. Westfield IIC (2010) 187 Cal.App.4th 469, 497.) Defendants' motion to strike Plaintiff's claims for Bee Tokens and stock is denied, as the motion is an improper attempt to adjudicate the merits of the case and is not brought on a proper ground. (See Code Civ. Proc. § 436 ["irrelevant, false, or improper matter"].) Likewise, Defendants' motions to strike Exhibits C and D is denied, as Defendants fail to set forth any basis as to why the exhibits are "irrelevant." Defendants' motion to strike the allegations regarding Mr. Chou is denied, as those allegations are relevant to Plaintiff's fraud cause of action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JORDAN ONG VS. BEENEST, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, September 23, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANT BEENEST, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION, JONATHAN CHOU DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' demurrer is overruled. The demurrer to the second and third causes of action on grounds of uncertainty is groundless. (See Lickiss v. Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 1125, 1135 [demurrers for uncertainty are disfavored, and are granted only if the pleading is so incomprehensible that a defendant cannot reasonably respond].) Defendants' demurrer to the second cause of action for breach of the covenant of good faith and fair dealing is overruled. Plaintiff has sufficiently pled that Defendants unfairly interfered with his right to receive the benefits of the various alleged agreements by terminating his employment and failing to perform their obligations under those agreements. (FAC 44.) Defendants' demurrer to the third cause of action for fraud is overruled. Plaintiff has pled with sufficient particularity each element of a claim for fraud. "An action for promissory fraud may lie where a defendant fraudulently induces the plaintiff to enter into a contract." (Lazar v. Superior Court (1996) 12 Cal.4th 631, 638.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
